items.q: Why is high IQ a problem for the statistical infrequency definition?
items.correct: It is statistically rare but not abnormal/undesirable.
items.misconception: Because high IQ is actually common.
items.why: Students miss that statistical infrequency lacks a value judgement - it just measures rarity. High IQ is rare but desirable, exposing the definition's limitation.

items.q: How does a securely attached infant respond to reunion with the caregiver?
items.correct: Seeks comfort and is easily soothed, then returns to play.
items.misconception: Shows no distress and ignores the caregiver.
items.random_wrong: Cries continuously and cannot be comforted.
items.why: Students confuse secure with avoidant. Secure infants DO show distress at separation and seek comfort at reunion - this contact-seeking is the marker of security, not indifference.

items.q: How do SSRIs help with OCD?
items.correct: They prevent reabsorption of serotonin, increasing its availability in the synapse.
items.misconception: They directly produce more serotonin in the brain.
items.why: Students think SSRIs create serotonin. They actually inhibit REUPTAKE - serotonin already released stays in the synapse longer, increasing post-synaptic stimulation.
The answer examiners want is: Frustration-aggression. “Social learning” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Loss of personal identity in a crowd is:
items.why: Correct answer: Deindividuation. “Compliance” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Dollard's frustration-aggression hypothesis was proposed in what year?
items.why: Exact answer: 1939. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
